deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explains why it is guiding rather than simply answering—no statement about active reasoning, retention, or skill development is offered. The tutor enforces a Socratic approach throughout but never articulates the pedagogical rationale to the student.

In Transcript 1 (Turn 10) and Transcript 2 (Turn 10), after the student makes clear conceptual progress from earlier confusion, the tutor moves directly to the next challenge without prompting the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why the improved approach worked.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts the tutor maintains a strong Socratic approach, consistently refusing to give direct answers or submission-ready content, scaffolding well from simpler to harder cases, and keeping key idea generation student-led. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring method and missed opportunities to prompt reflection after student breakthroughs.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions warranted. The tutor never provides final answers, never rewrites student text, and never states conclusions before the student reasons toward them. Even when the clueless student repeatedly asks for full explanations, the tutor redirects with targeted questions. 1.2: Scaffolding is consistently strong—the tutor builds on each correct student step, moves from simple to complex cases, and diagnoses confusion before responding. 1.3: Two deductions apply. First, the tutoring rationale is never explained to the student in any of the three conversations (1.3.A.a, -2 pts). Second, after students make clear progress from confusion to a defensible argument (e.g., Transcript 1 Turn 10, Transcript 2 Turn 10, Transcript 3 Turn 10), the tutor always pivots to the next challenge without prompting reflection on what changed and why it worked (1.3.C.a, -2 pts). Method-level feedback is present and specific throughout, so no deduction for 1.3.B. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: no repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, responses are concise and well-targeted, and tone is consistently supportive and formative.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on the whole act consequentialism thing for this assignment tbh. can u just explain it from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
Sure—but you need to do the thinking from there. Start here: act consequentialism judges each individual act by its consequences. So the basic question is: of the available actions, which one leads to the best overall outcome?

For this assignment, what do you think an act consequentialist would say in a simple case where killing 1 prevents 5 deaths? Why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give a very brief conceptual starting point without writing the assignment for the student. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I’ll frame the core idea of act consequentialism in simple terms and then ask a focused question that moves directly toward applying it to the killing-one-to-save-five cases in the assignment. I will keep it bite-sized and avoid giving a full solution or essay language.

exchanges.student: uh probably they'd say kill the 1 bc 5 saved is better, but then i get confused w the trolley stuff rn. can u just walk me through all the cases?
exchanges.tutor:
That first step is fine: for an act consequentialist, saving 5 rather than 1 usually looks better.

Don’t try to do all the cases at once. Use one test for each case: what are the options, and which option has the best consequences overall?

Pick one case—Fuel, Blood, or a trolley case. In that case, what are the two main actions available, and what happens under each?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not walk through all cases or provide the assignment’s solution directly. Instead, I’ll validate the student’s initial grasp, then narrow to a reusable method for analyzing each case. This uses guided discovery, keeps the response short, and asks a Socratic question that advances toward the solution. I will avoid writing their argument for them.

exchanges.student: maybe trolley? i just mix them all up tbh, like push vs switch vs whatever and then Fuel/Blood too. can u explain the differences first bc it's all blurring together?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—separate them by structure, not by name.

Try this:
1. In one kind of case, you redirect a threat already in motion.
2. In another, you use a person as part of the rescue.
3. In others like Fuel/Blood, ask whether harming 1 is a side effect or the means.

Which cases seem to fit category 1 versus 2? Even a rough guess is enough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused among cases, so I should reduce cognitive load by helping them sort the cases conceptually rather than solve them. I will stay bite-sized, avoid giving conclusions, and use guided discovery by asking them to classify the cases by action type and consequence pattern. This supports the assignment while not writing it for them.

exchanges.student: ngl i really don't know, it all feels like ur still choosing 1 dies so 5 live. can u just explain from scratch how act consequentialism handles each type?
exchanges.tutor:
Here’s the key point: act consequentialism usually does not care much about the difference between redirecting, pushing, or using someone as a means if the outcome is the same. It asks: which act produces the best overall consequences?

So ask yourself: if two cases both end with 1 dead and 5 saved, would an act consequentialist treat them differently? Why or why not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a direct walkthrough, but I must avoid providing full solutions. I’ll clarify the core principle again, then prompt them to apply it to one familiar contrast that moves toward the assignment’s deeper issue: whether act consequentialism distinguishes cases at all. This is concise, Socratic, and keeps focus on the problem.

exchanges.student: i guess no, they wouldn't treat them differently if the numbers are the same. but then why do ppl think push is worse than switch fr?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly—that’s the pressure point in the assignment.

Many people think push is worse because the person is being used differently, not just because of the body count. What moral difference do you think they’re noticing between switching a track and pushing a person?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should build on the student's correct inference and pivot to the assignment's evaluative part: whether act consequentialism gets the cases right. I'll avoid answering directly by asking the student to articulate the moral feature people appeal to in distinguishing push from switch. This is a focused Socratic move toward the solution and remains concise.

exchanges.student: maybe that in push ur like directly doing it to them, but i'm still confused how that connects to Fuel and Blood tbh. can u tie all of them together?
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now test that idea across all the cases.

For each case, ask two things:
1. Is the one person’s death a means to saving the five, or just a side effect?
2. Is the agent initiating harm or redirecting an existing threat?

Take Fuel or Blood: which of those two questions seems most important there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a promising distinction ('directly doing it'), but is asking to have all cases tied together. I should not supply the full synthesis. Instead, I’ll help them build a unifying comparison framework that they can apply across Fuel, Blood, and trolley cases. This uses guided discovery, stays concise, and keeps the student actively reasoning rather than passively receiving a solution.

exchanges.student: idk maybe means vs side effect?? but i'm not sure at all rn, can u just give me the overall framework for all of them?
exchanges.tutor:
Fine—the framework is this:

First: what would act consequentialism say? Usually: choose the act with the best outcome.
Second: what do our case-judgments suggest? That outcome may not be the whole story.
Third: what extra principle might matter? Common candidates are means vs side effect, intending harm, or rights/constraints on killing.

So which part of your paper is hardest: stating the act consequentialist view, applying it to the cases, or arguing it gets some cases wrong?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck and asking for the overall framework. I should provide a minimal organizing framework without turning it into a solution. I will give a compact map of the assignment's moving parts, then immediately ask the student to apply it. This remains Socratic, avoids complete answers, and helps them move forward instead of circling.

exchanges.student: probably the last part, like arguing it's wrong without rambling. i need help making that part make sense.
exchanges.tutor:
Then make it a 3-step argument, not a ramble:

1. What verdict does act consequentialism give across these cases?
2. Which pair of cases seems morally different to you despite similar outcomes?
3. What principle explains that difference better than act consequentialism?

Pick one pair first—push vs switch, or Blood vs Fuel. Which pair gives you the clearest contrast?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now identifies the specific difficulty: critiquing act consequentialism concisely. I should help them structure the argument without supplying content they can copy. Following Socratic and guided discovery, I’ll provide a tight argumentative template in question form and ask them to fill it with their own claims. This stays within the assignment, avoids writing for them, and keeps the response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: push vs switch prob, bc that one's more obvious to me. i just can't say the principle cleanly tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Then don’t hunt for perfect wording yet—identify the idea first.

In push, is the person’s death part of how the five are saved, or does it just happen alongside the rescue? In switch, is that true in the same way?

Answer those two questions plainly, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has chosen a useful comparison and is asking for help formulating the principle. I must not write or suggest language for them, so I will avoid giving a clean sentence they can use. Instead, I’ll force them to articulate the contrast in their own words through targeted prompts. This keeps the interaction Socratic, concise, and directly aimed at the assignment’s final argumentative step.

exchanges.student: i think in push the person's death is part of the plan, but in switch it's more like u reroute it and they die bc of that. i'm not sure if that's the right way to say it tho.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s close enough conceptually. Now do the philosophical work: if push involves the one person’s death as part of the rescue plan, but switch does not in the same way, why is that a problem for act consequentialism?

One more step: does that suggest we need a rule against using a person’s death as a means, even when the numbers look better?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the key distinction. I should affirm the progress without polishing their wording, and push them to connect that distinction to the critique of act consequentialism. To follow the rules, I must not supply a final formulation or write their sentence, so I will ask them to draw the philosophical implication themselves. Keep it brief and solution-directed.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a philosophy question about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five. I think act consequentialism says it depends on the overall outcome, but I’m not totally sure how that applies to cases like the trolley examples.
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. You’ve identified the key thought: act consequentialism judges each act by its consequences. Now make that more precise: what would the theory tell you to compare in a case where one dies and five live?

Pick one case first—say a standard trolley case or Fuel. Under act consequentialism, which action has the best overall outcome, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's starting point, then use a focused Socratic question to move them from a vague statement about outcomes to applying act consequentialism to the specific cases named in the assignment. I will not give conclusions for the cases; instead, I will prompt the student to articulate the decision rule and test it on one example. This keeps the response brief, on-task, and centered on guided discovery.

exchanges.student: I think it would compare the total consequences of each option, like how many people live or die overall. So in a standard trolley case, I’m guessing act consequentialism would say I should pull the lever, because one person dies instead of five, which seems like the better outcome.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—likely the lever-pull looks favored on that view. But be careful: is act consequentialism always just a body-counting theory, or does it ask about overall value more broadly?

Now test your rule on a harder case from Lecture 13—maybe Blood or a different trolley case. Would the same reasoning still approve killing one to save five there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's progress without giving the full solution, then push them to refine their understanding by distinguishing act consequentialism's core metric from a simplistic headcount and by extending their reasoning to another case from the assignment. This advances them toward comparing cases and evaluating whether the view gets them right.

exchanges.student: I think it’s broader than just counting deaths — it’s supposed to look at overall good and bad consequences. But I’m stuck on Blood a bit; I’m not sure if the idea is still just “save the greater number,” or if killing the one directly makes act consequentialism still say yes anyway.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re circling the key issue. Ask yourself: in Blood, what changes from the standard trolley case—only the route to the outcome, or the outcome itself?

If the totals are the same, would an act consequentialist treat “directly causing” the one death as morally important? Should it? Give me your own comparison in 2–3 sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the student focused on the assignment by asking them to isolate the morally relevant features of Blood and compare them with the standard trolley case. I will not supply the verdict. The question is designed to help them see that act consequentialism may treat directness as irrelevant if outcomes are the same, which they need to articulate themselves before evaluating whether that seems right.

exchanges.student: I think if the outcome is still one death versus five deaths, then an act consequentialist probably wouldn’t treat the fact that I caused the death more directly as important by itself. So it seems like it would still approve killing the one if that really leads to saving five. I’m just not sure whether that feels right, because direct killing seems morally worse even if the totals match.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You’ve now got the tension the paper is about: the theory may say yes, but your moral judgment may resist it.

So what feature do you think matters besides total outcome—intention, using a person as a means, doing vs allowing, or something else? Which one best explains why some one-for-five cases feel permissible and others do not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's progress and move them toward the next assignment component: evaluating whether act consequentialism gets the cases right and sketching an alternative general account. I will use Socratic questions that force them to identify a principle distinguishing cases without drafting the answer for them.

exchanges.student: I think “using a person as a means” seems like the strongest distinction. My guess is that in some trolley-type cases the one person’s death is a side effect, but in others the person is used as part of the plan to save the five, and that’s why those cases feel less permissible to me.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong candidate. Now pressure-test it: in which Lecture 13 cases is the one person merely foreseen to die, and in which is that person part of the saving strategy?

Try sorting two cases into those categories. Then ask: does your principle match your intuitions better than act consequentialism does?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's promising distinction and guide them to test it against the assignment's cases. This uses Socratic questioning to help them build a general account themselves, without supplying conclusions or wording for the paper. I will keep it brief and focused on applying the principle to specific examples.

exchanges.student: I think in the standard trolley case, the one person’s death seems more like a foreseen side effect of diverting the trolley. But in a case like pushing the large man, he’s part of the strategy, since his body is what stops the trolley. That distinction matches my intuition better than act consequentialism, because act consequentialism seems to treat both as okay if the numbers work out.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now move from examples to a rule. What would your general account say is the condition for permissibility: is it okay only when the one person’s death is a side effect rather than a means?

Before you commit, test that rule on one more case from Lecture 13, like Fuel or Blood. Does it still hold?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reinforce the student's progress and push them to formulate the assignment's required 'right general account' in their own terms. I will avoid giving a polished thesis or sentence-level writing, and instead ask a targeted question that helps them turn examples into a principle with conditions and limits.

exchanges.student: I think my rule would be something like: it can be morally okay to kill one to save five only when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect, not when the person is intentionally used as a means. I’m a little unsure about Fuel or Blood, though — I think one of them might be tricky because the death feels more intentional even if it still helps save the five.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly—that’s where your view needs sharpening. Don’t ask yet whether the case is “tricky”; ask one narrower question: in that case, is the victim’s death part of the plan, or merely predicted as a cost of the plan?

Choose Fuel or Blood and answer just that question first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not resolve Fuel or Blood for the student. Instead, I will narrow the task to identifying one morally relevant feature in the tricky case and comparing it to the student's proposed rule. This keeps the conversation bite-sized, Socratic, and moving toward a defensible general account.

exchanges.student: I’ll pick Blood. I think the victim’s death is part of the plan there, not just a predicted cost, because the person’s body or death is being used to produce the saving outcome.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Then take the next step: if Blood uses the person as a means, what would act consequentialism say, and what would your view say?

Answer both, then tell me which seems more plausible to you and why. Keep it to 3 short sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s analytic move and guide them to connect it back to the assignment’s three parts: act consequentialism’s verdict, whether that verdict is plausible, and the student’s alternative principle. I will ask for a compact synthesis rather than provide one, keeping the student doing the reasoning and writing.

exchanges.student: I think act consequentialism would still say Blood is permissible if it leads to the best overall outcome, like one death instead of five. My view would say it’s not permissible, because the person is being used as a means to save the five. That seems more plausible to me because it explains why direct instrumental killing feels worse than causing a death as a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the core argument. Now integrate it.

Can you sketch a 3-part structure: (1) what act consequentialism says, (2) how it handles the lecture cases, and (3) why your side-effect/means distinction does better? Give me bullet points only, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed the core contrast needed for the assignment. I should now push them to integrate their reasoning into a compact structure for the essay without writing it for them. To follow the rules, I will not supply a thesis paragraph or polished language; instead I will ask for a brief outline that covers all assignment parts and moves them toward a complete response.

exchanges.student:
- Act consequentialism judges each act by which one has the best overall consequences, so in one-versus-five cases it will usually favor killing one if that saves five.  
- In the lecture cases, that means it tends to treat standard trolley, Blood, and similar cases alike whenever the outcome is better overall, and it doesn’t give much weight to whether the one person is directly used.  
- I think that misses an important moral difference: cases where the one person’s death is a side effect seem more permissible than cases where the person is used as a means, and that distinction fits my intuitions better than act consequentialism does.
exchanges.tutor:
Good structure. The weak point is your last bullet: “fits my intuitions better” is a start, but not yet much of an argument.

So ask yourself: why should “using as a means” matter morally? What principle makes that difference important, beyond just your reaction to the cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid high-level structure. I should give concise evaluative feedback without grading, note one conceptual pressure point to deepen the argument, and prompt them to refine rather than provide wording. This stays within the tutor role, uses guided discovery, and avoids writing the answer for them.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help with my philosophy assignment about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five. I’m pretty confused about how an act consequentialist would handle the trolley-style cases, especially Fuel and Blood.
exchanges.tutor:
Start with the simplest lens: for an act consequentialist, what makes an act right or wrong in a particular case?

Try to state that principle in one sentence, then test it on Fuel and Blood: in each case, what are the options, and which option seems to produce the best overall outcome? If you want, give me your one-sentence principle first, and we’ll pressure-test it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should stay in the tutor role, keep the response brief, and use guided discovery rather than giving conclusions. The student is asking about the core concepts of the assignment, so I will narrow the task, ask a solution-directed question, and invite the student to compare cases using the act consequentialist standard. This helps them build their own argument without me supplying a ready-made answer or wording.

exchanges.student: I think the one-sentence principle is: an act is right if, in that specific situation, it leads to the best overall consequences compared to the alternatives. So for Fuel and Blood, I’m guessing the act consequentialist just compares outcomes and says killing one to save five is right if that produces more total good, but I’m not fully sure how Blood is supposed to differ.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That principle is the right starting point.

Now don’t stay abstract—compare the cases. In Blood, is the one person’s death a side effect, or is that person being used as a means to save the five? And would an act consequentialist care about that distinction if the totals come out the same?

Pick one case and tell me: what are the options, and what outcome ranking would the act consequentialist give them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the core act consequentialist principle well. I should affirm that progress briefly, then push them to distinguish the cases by identifying the mechanism of harm and the available alternatives. To avoid giving the answer directly, I will ask targeted comparative questions that guide them toward whether act consequentialism treats the cases differently and whether that seems plausible.

exchanges.student: Okay, I think in Blood the person is being used more directly as a means, not just as a side effect. But I’m guessing an act consequentialist wouldn’t care about that distinction if the result is still 1 dead instead of 5 dead. So the ranking would just be: save five / one dies is better than do nothing / five die, even if that feels kind of harsh.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—now you’re at the real pressure point.

If act consequentialism treats means and side effects the same, what does it say about the different trolley cases overall? Do your own judgments line up with that, or do you think some cases should come out differently?

Name one case where your intuition resists the act consequentialist result, and say why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the means/side-effect distinction and correctly connected it to act consequentialism’s likely indifference to that distinction. I should now push them toward the next assignment component: whether act consequentialism gets the cases right. To preserve guided discovery, I will ask them to articulate the tension between theory and intuition and compare multiple trolley-style cases without supplying a conclusion.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think my intuition resists the case where someone is used as a means, like pushing a person or maybe Blood if that’s the structure. Act consequentialism seems to say it’s fine whenever it saves more people, but that feels wrong to me because it seems like there should be some moral difference between redirecting a threat and intentionally making someone the thing that gets harmed.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now turn that intuition into a rule.

What general principle could explain why redirecting a threat might be permissible, while intentionally using a person as the tool of rescue is not?

Don’t worry about perfect wording. Just propose the core idea: what feature makes the moral difference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the central tension: aggregate outcomes versus the means/side-effect distinction. I should help them move from intuition to argument by asking them to formulate a candidate principle that explains why some killings are permissible and others are not, without giving them a finished theory or wording. Keep it brief and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: Maybe the core idea is that it’s more acceptable to harm someone as a foreseen side effect than to intentionally use that person as the means of saving others. I’m not sure if that’s basically the doctrine of double effect, but I think that’s the kind of distinction I want.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a promising direction. Now test it instead of just naming it.

Apply your distinction to two cases: one where killing seems permissible and one where it seems impermissible. How would your principle classify each, and does it match your intuition better than act consequentialism does?

Try Fuel and Blood first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at the key distinction and tentatively identified double effect. I should affirm the direction but avoid naming it as the answer in a conclusive way. The next step is to test whether their proposed principle can handle the assignment’s cases and whether it improves on act consequentialism. I will ask them to apply it to concrete cases and assess its strengths and limits.

exchanges.student: I think my principle would say Fuel is more likely permissible if the one person’s death is a side effect of redirecting the threat, while Blood is impermissible if the person has to be used as the means to save the five. That matches my intuition better than act consequentialism, because act consequentialism flattens both cases into the same math and I don’t think they feel morally the same.
exchanges.tutor:
Strong progress. But right now you have an intuition plus a distinction—you still need a defense.

Why should “using a person as a means” matter morally? What value is being protected there: rights, inviolability, respect for persons, something else?

Pick one and explain how it helps justify your general account.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a substantive comparative claim. I should now guide them toward strengthening the philosophical argument by asking for justification beyond intuition. This advances the assignment’s requirement to assess whether act consequentialism gets the cases right and to offer a better general account. I will ask a focused why-question and invite them to identify what value their principle protects.

exchanges.student: I think rights or maybe respect for persons makes the most sense. My rough thought is that when someone is used as a means, they’re being treated like a tool instead of as a person with their own moral status, and that seems like a violation that shouldn’t be justified just by adding up benefits to others.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That gives you the backbone of the paper.

Now organize it: what are your 3 main moves?
1) what act consequentialism says,
2) why that seems wrong in some cases,
3) your better general account.

Give me a 1-line summary of each part in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has the core argumentative materials: act consequentialism, objection, and alternative principle grounded in rights/respect. I should prompt them to assemble these into the structure of the assignment rather than adding new concepts. To avoid doing the writing for them, I will ask for a concise outline in their own words.

exchanges.student: Okay, I think part 1 is: act consequentialism says the right act is whichever produces the best overall outcome, so it would allow killing one to save five in both Fuel and Blood if the totals are better. Part 2 is: that seems wrong in cases where the one person is used as a means, because my intuition is that those cases are morally worse than cases where death is just a side effect. Part 3 is: a better account is that killing one to save five is only okay when the person is not intentionally used as a tool, which fits better with rights or respect for persons.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid structure. One weakness: part 3 may be too quick.

How would your view handle a hard case where harming one as a side effect still feels manipulated or unfair? In other words, what limit keeps your principle from being too broad?

Try to state one constraint your view would add.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has assembled a solid three-part structure. I should acknowledge progress, but continue using guided discovery by pressing on a likely weakness: the student is leaning heavily on intuition and needs to clarify scope and handle an objection. I will ask a focused question that moves directly toward a stronger thesis without supplying phrasing or content for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe I need a constraint that it’s not enough for the death to be a side effect; the harm also can’t be deliberately imposed on an innocent person just because they’re available. I’m still fuzzy on how to word that, but I think the idea is that redirecting a threat can be different from selecting someone to bear the cost.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a real improvement. Keep the idea, but you must phrase it yourself.

Test your distinction like this: what is the moral difference between redirecting an existing threat and choosing an innocent person to absorb the harm? If you can explain that clearly in 2–3 sentences, you’ll have your concluding claim.

Write that explanation here in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close to a workable position and is asking about wording, but the rules forbid me from drafting language for them. I should keep them on-task, affirm the conceptual advance, and ask them to articulate the distinction in their own terms with a concrete case test. That preserves Socratic guidance and avoids writing the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: I think the difference is that redirecting an existing threat doesn’t make the innocent person into the object chosen for sacrifice in the same way; their death is a tragic result of stopping a greater harm. But choosing an innocent person to absorb the harm treats that person as if they can be assigned the cost for everyone else’s benefit, which violates their rights and fails to respect them as an individual.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s a defensible core argument.

Main strength: you now explain why act consequentialism seems too crude in Blood-type cases. Remaining task: make sure you explicitly say whether your view rejects act consequentialism altogether or just says it misses an important moral constraint.

Now stop circling and turn this into a full draft in your own words. If you want, paste your intro or first paragraph next.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed a coherent position with a distinction, a criticism of act consequentialism, and a rights/respect-based alternative. I should not write the assignment for them or polish their prose. Following the rules, I will give a brief evaluative response without grading, note the main strength and one remaining task, and push them to integrate what we discussed into a full draft attempt since the conversation has now circled around the same core idea several times.
